retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Peptide -- Molecular Level -- Peptide Hormones
retrieved_docs.content: Calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) is a 37-amino acid neuropeptide, which is most commonly localized to C and Aδ sensory fibers but affects both the central and peripheral nervous systems and metabolism through a multitude of receptor types. [16] New research has demonstrated that intraperitoneal treatment with CGRP can have an energy stimulating effect and even promote an increased appetite. [17] Current research has also linked the activity CGRP in the cerebrovascular system to a possible etiology of migraine attacks. [18] CGRP is mainly found in the enteric nervous system but has been postulated to play a potential role in cranial nociception and cerebral vasodilation, leading to severe migraine headaches. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Similarly, newer studies find that CGRP is also involved in several other physiological/pathological phenomena, such as peripheral nerve regeneration, Alzheimer disease, vascular tone of mesenteric arteries, and pregnancy. However, no drugs have been developed which take advantage of these effects of CGRP.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Summarize the role of RAMP1 in the formation of the CGRP receptor. Outline the currently FDA-approved drugs acting on CGRP or its receptor. Identify the most common adverse events associated with CGRP receptor antagonists. Review the importance of proper monitoring and coordination amongst interprofessional team members to improve outcomes for patients affected by migraine. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Eptinezumab is a humanized immunoglobulin G1 produced in Pichia pastoris yeast cells by recombinant DNA technology. Eptinezumab potently and selectively binds to the α and β-forms of the human CGRP ligand and prevents the activation of the CGRP receptor. It has a rapid onset of action and prolonged clinical activity. This action may be due to the rapid binding and inactivation of CGRP and slow dissociation from the peptide. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: To understand the mechanism of action, some information on the anatomy of the CGRP receptors may prove useful. The CGRP receptor is a heteromeric receptor complex composed of three subunits:

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: CGRP receptor antagonists ("gepants") are the first class of migraine-specific medication that does not have vasoconstrictive action, shows similar efficacy, has fewer adverse effects, and has a possibly longer period of activity than triptans. [7] Gepants demonstrate an extremely high affinity for CGRP receptors of human and non-human primates, preventing in this way the interaction between CGRP and its receptor. [12] The structured N-terminal extracellular (ECD) domain of CLR serves as a binding site for gepants, thus providing its fast-acting antagonistic action. The currently FDA-approved gepants are rimegepant and ubrogepant.

retrieved_docs.title: Migraine With Aura -- Pathophysiology -- Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide
retrieved_docs.content: CGRP is a neuropeptide expressed in trigeminal ganglia nerves that serves as a potent vasodilator of cerebral and dural vessels and plays an essential role in the pathogenesis of migraine. [10] [11] CGRP mediates pain transmission from the intracranial vessels to the central nervous system and is involved in the vasodilatory component of neurogenic inflammation. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Fremanezumab is a fully humanized IgG2 produced by recombinant DNA technology in Chinese hamster ovary (CHO) cells. Fremanezumab selectively targets and binds to both the α and β isoforms of CGRP and prevents CGRP-induced receptor activation. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Since the studies regarding CGRP and its receptor, physiology, and pharmacology were conducted relatively recently, there is much unknown about it. The new drugs acting on the CGRP receptor have greater efficacy and advantages over the drugs currently used for the treatment of migraines, which may lead to the widespread use of these new drugs. However, professionals within the field of internal medicine and neurology have to take care while prescribing these drugs and properly inform patients about the potential side effects of these medications. Watching for adverse effects reported in previous studies and preparing for those that have not yet been described is also vital.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: These monoclonal antibodies act rapidly and prevent the binding of CGRP. This receptor blockade is long-term, which leads to a reliable method of migraine prophylaxis using only a few doses of the drug per month. These drugs are degraded into small peptides and amino acids by enzymatic proteolysis before excretion, as with other monoclonal antibodies.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Calcitonin gene-related peptide receptors (CGRP) receptor antagonists and monoclonal antibodies against CGRP and its receptor are medications used in the management and treatment of migraine, which is the sixth-highest cause worldwide of years lost due to disability as per the Global Burden of Disease Study (2013). This activity describes the indications, actions, and contraindications for these drugs as valuable agents in the prophylaxis and acute management of migraine and cluster headaches. This activity will highlight the mechanism of action, monitoring, adverse events, relevant interactions, and other key elements in the clinical setting as relates to the essential points needed by members of an interprofessional team managing the care of patients with migraines.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: In the central nervous system, researchers observed that a raised level of blood and salivary levels of CGRP occur in patients with headache disorders such as migraines and cluster headaches and neuralgias such as trigeminal neuralgia, chronic paroxysmal hemicranias, and even rhinosinusitis. Levels are elevated during a migraine attack and between migraine attacks in patients with chronic migraines. Also, CGRP from exogenous infusions was shown to trigger migraine attacks. [2] Later studies showed that CGRP released in the trigeminal neurons was associated with the release of vasoactive neuropeptides and vasodilation of the cerebral vasculature, thus playing a role in the pathogenesis of migraine.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: The first four drugs listed are monoclonal antibodies used in the prophylaxis of chronic and episodic migraine.

retrieved_docs.title: Ergotamine/Caffeine  -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Although the cause of migraine headaches is not entirely understood, the thinking is that arteriolar vasodilation and inflammation of the trigeminal nerve due to the release of local vasoactive peptides, such as 5-hydroxytryptamine (5-HT) and calcitonin gene-related protein (CGRP) plays a role. CGRP is a neuropeptide that can be found in the central and peripheral nervous systems and has pain-signaling and vasodilator functions. Patients with migraines have elevated levels of circulating CGRP, and migraine medications, such as the ergot alkaloids, are associated with lowering these CGRP levels. [2] [4] Ergotamine has a complex mechanism of action that involves a variety of receptors, including 5-HT-1B/1D, dopamine, and alpha-adrenoreceptors. By activating 5HT-1B and 5HT-1D receptors on intracranial blood vessels, ergotamine induces vasoconstriction and relief of migraine headaches. Ergotamine also inhibits norepinephrine uptake and stimulates alpha-adrenergic receptors at therapeutic doses, leading to prolonged vasoconstriction. Blood flow to the extremities thus becomes reduced. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Migraine Medications -- Function -- Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide (CGRP)
retrieved_docs.content: CGRP monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) are the only class of currently used preventives explicitly developed for the treatment of migraines. The current thinking is that CGRP mediates the vasodilatory component of neurogenic inflammation, as CGRP is a widely distributed vasodilator. The CGRP mAbs target either the CGRP molecule itself or the CGRP receptor. In network meta-analysis, the CGRP mAbs seemed to be as effective as other preventive treatments, but have fewer side effects. [14] Long-term data on safety, however, is limited.  These medications include erenumab, fremanezumab, and galcanezumab.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The monoclonal antibodies (erenumab, eptinezumab, galcanezumab, fremanezumab) are used for migraine prophylaxis in episodic and chronic migraine. Rimegepant and ubrogepant are used for acute treatment of migraine with or without aura. galcanezumab and fremanezumab are under development for the preventive treatment of cluster headaches.

retrieved_docs.content: same unspecified CNS disorder, underscoring the wide neurovascular context in which migraine can occur. Overall, migraine (شقيقة) is interconnected with hereditary, vascular, and structural cerebral disorders, is classified across several hierarchical and synonymous relationships, and serves as both a specific clinical entity and a bridge to broader central nervous system disease concepts, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, genetic counseling, and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2186
retrieved_docs.content: Headache, including migraine, is classified as a condition that falls under the broader category of nervous system and sense organ diseases. This hierarchical relationship indicates that headaches and migraines are considered manifestations of disorders affecting the nervous system and sensory organs, highlighting their relevance within the spectrum of neurological and sensory pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1062
retrieved_docs.content: within nervous system disorders. Clinically, شقيقة and شقيقة فالجية عائلية are considered clinically similar, and شقيقة and صداع وعائي are described as related and possibly synonymous, suggesting substantial overlap in their clinical presentation and underlying mechanisms. Together, these relationships integrate migraine into a network of genetic, vascular, and systemic neurological conditions, underscore its diagnostic and therapeutic considerations, and illustrate its connections to both broader headache classifications and specific hereditary migraine phenotypes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2239
retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhage into nerve trunk, inflammatory and toxic neuropathy, and hereditary sensory neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب الحسي الوراثي) are all conditions in which the nerve serves as the primary site of pathological findings. This highlights that the nerve is a common locus for diverse neuropathic processes, ranging from acute hemorrhagic injury to inflammatory, toxic, and genetic sensory neuropathies. Recognizing the nerve as the affected structure helps clinicians focus diagnostic evaluation and tailor management strategies for patients presenting with these nerve‑related disorders.

retrieved_docs.content: anamnesis of nervous‑system and sensory diseases and are linked with associated findings of migraine itself, reinforcing the importance of thorough documentation. Overall, migraine occupies a pivotal position at the intersection of pain, headache syndromes, vascular and hereditary neurological disorders, and psychosomatic health, highlighting its multifaceted impact on diagnosis, management, and patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 413

Overall, the integrated knowledge base links migraine (شقيقة) to its hierarchical placement under headache and pain, its clinical overlap with vascular and hereditary headache disorders, its manifestation in specific cerebrovascular diseases, and its prevention with topiramat, thereby providing a comprehensive framework for understanding and managing migraine within the spectrum of nervous system disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 245
retrieved_docs.content: reflecting the common view of migraine as a vascular‑origin headache. Procedural relationships are also described. The surgical concept “incision and excision of CNS” is linked to “lebka” (the skull), which in turn is related to “اضطراب بالعصب القحفي” (cranial‑nerve disorder) and to “other non‑or or closed therapeutic nervous‑system procedures.” These links suggest that interventions involving the skull may be relevant for treating cranial‑nerve disorders that can present with migraine‑like symptoms. Overall, the collected triples portray migraine (شقيقة) as a central node that intersects with chronic headache syndromes, hereditary migraine forms, cerebrovascular arteriopathies, ocular migraine subtypes, and broader vascular‑headache classifications, while also being connected to specific neurosurgical procedures and general nervous‑system disorder categories. This integrated view supports comprehensive patient assessment and multidisciplinary management of migraine and its related

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 194
retrieved_docs.content: underscoring the extensive network of conditions—genetic, vascular, developmental, traumatic, neoplastic, and CSF‑related—that converge on migraine as a central node within neurological health and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2198
retrieved_docs.content: Hereditary disorder of nervous system is a broad category of genetic conditions affecting the nervous system, and شقيقة فالجية عائلية (familial hemiplegic migraine) is a specific instance within this category. شقيقة فالجية عائلية represents a hereditary form of migraine that is characterized by transient hemiplegia and other neurological symptoms, illustrating how a hereditary disorder of the nervous system can manifest as a distinct migraine subtype.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2775
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is recognized as an etiological factor for several neurological conditions. It is identified as a cause of الخرف من نوع الزهايمر (Alzheimer‑type dementia), of headache—including migraine—, of nervous system congenital anomalies, and of نزيف دموي تحت الجافية (subdural hemorrhage). Recognizing that the same allowed qualifier can underlie these diverse disorders aids clinicians in understanding their pathogenesis, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing targeted therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.content: MIGRAINE, FAMILIAL HEMIPLEGIC, 2 is characterized by the manifestation of حُبسة, indicating that individuals with this hereditary hemiplegic migraine subtype commonly experience the clinical feature described by the term حُبسة.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2159
retrieved_docs.content: Migraine and Headache Syndromes are categorized as a subcategory of the broader condition known as اعتلال عصبي عضلي (neuromuscular disease). This hierarchical relationship suggests that migraine and headache disorders may be viewed within the context of neuromuscular pathology, highlighting the importance of considering underlying neuromuscular factors when diagnosing and managing patients presenting with these headache syndromes.

Collectively, these concepts demonstrate how neuroanatomical structures, cognitive and emotional functions, imaging modalities, neurosurgical shunt procedures, and a spectrum of vascular, inflammatory, toxic, and traumatic brain disorders interrelate to inform comprehensive diagnosis, risk assessment, and management in neurology, neurosurgery, and neuro‑critical care.
